coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots enhanced ultrathin nanofilms and arginine engineered nanofiltration membranes with ultra-high separation performance
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jiawei
Wang, Zhen
Li, Wenwen
Wang, Xuliang
Su, Yanlei
coredata.dc:description: A noticeable improvement of nanofiltration (NF) membranes with high separation performance for desalination was reported in this work. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) enhanced polyamide (PA) composite membranes (GPA) were composed of porous substrates and nanofilms The nanofilms as separation layers were fabricated by the support-free interfacial polymerization and possessed ultrathin thickness below 8.0 nm. GQDs-PA interface channels and GQDs-GQDs interface channels gradually generated and penetrated the PA functional layer, resulting in a dramatic permeance increase of GPA membranes. The GPA membranes were creatively modified with arginine (Arg) to improve hydrophilicity and narrow the water channels. The Arg-GPA NF membranes under optimized preparation exhibited exceedingly high permeance of 40.3 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 with Na2SO4 rejection rate of 99.7 %, and 60.7 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 with Na2SO4 rejection rate of 99.4 %. This synergistic strategy of GQDs regulation on PA nanofilm structure and Arg post-treatment modification may provide a reliable and efficient technique for the design of NF membranes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Support-free interfacial polymerization
Arginine modification
Nanofiltration
Ultra-high separation performance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of GPA and Arg-GPA membranes
Separation performance of GPA and Arg-GPA membranes
Structure characterization and permselectivity of GPA membranes
Structure characterization and properties of the Arg-GPA membranes
Permeance and selectivity of Arg-GPA NF composite membranes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of synthetic process and interfacial channels for water transport in the GPA NF composite membrane.

The structure and separation performance of GPA membranes. a) and d) SEM image of GPA composite membranes. b), c), e) and f) Topography image and AFM height image of the GPA nanofilms transferred onto silicon wafer. g) Thickness and roughness of GPA nanofilms with different GQDs content. h) Permeance and inorganic salt rejection rate of GPA composite membranes.
Schematic diagram of Arg modification reaction and chemical grafting of Arg-GPA membrane.

The morphological characterization, elementary composition and state, and surface properties of GPA1 and Arg-GPA1-4 membranes. a) SEM image of Arg-GPA1-4 membrane. b) and c) Topography image and AFM height image of Arg-GPA1-4 membrane transferred onto silicon wafer. d) XPS spectra of GPA1 membrane. Inset: element composition. e) XPS C1s spectra of GPA1 membrane. f) XPS spectra of Arg-GPA1-4 membrane. Inset: element composition. g) XPS C1s spectra of Arg-GPA1-4 membrane. h) MWCOs of GPA1 and Arg-GPA1-4 membranes. Inset: Average pore diameter and pore diameter distribution. i) Zeta potential and water contact angle of GPA1 and Arg-GPA1-4 membranes.

Separation performance and long-term stability of Arg-GPA membranes. a) and b) Separation performance of the Arg-GPA1-4 and Arg-GPA2-1 membranes under pressure cycling experiment by feeding 1000 ppm Na2SO4 solution. c) and d) The separation performance of Arg-GPA1-4 and Arg-GPA2-1 membranes at solutions of different Na2SO4 concentrations. d) Comparison of permeance and Na2SO4 rejection between Arg-GPA1-4 membrane and reported NF membranes in literature. f) Long-term stability of Arg-GPA1-4 NF composite membrane in 120-h separate operation.
High performance of the Arg engineered nanofilms.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Arg-GPA membranes were prepared by soaking into Arg solution with different reaction time and different Arg concentration at 60 °C for post-treatment modification.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots enhanced ultrathin nanofilms and arginine engineered nanofiltration membranes with ultra-high separation performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Technology of Ministry of Education, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Institute of Seawater Desalination and Multipurpose Utilization, NMR (Tianjin), Tianjin 300192, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Institute of Seawater Desalination and Multipurpose Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Institute of Seawater Desalination and Multipurpose Utilization, NMR(Tianjin), Tianjin 300192, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, No. 135, Yaguan Road, Jinnan District, Tianjin 300350, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A noticeable improvement of nanofiltration (NF) membranes with high separation performance for desalination was reported in this work. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) enhanced polyamide (PA) composite membranes (GPA) were composed of porous substrates and nanofilms The nanofilms as separation layers were fabricated by the support-free interfacial polymerization and possessed ultrathin thickness below 8.0 nm. GQDs-PA interface channels and GQDs-GQDs interface channels gradually generated and penetrated the PA functional layer, resulting in a dramatic permeance increase of GPA membranes. The GPA membranes were creatively modified with arginine (Arg) to improve hydrophilicity and narrow the water channels. The Arg-GPA NF membranes under optimized preparation exhibited exceedingly high permeance of 40.3 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 with Na2SO4 rejection rate of 99.7 %, and 60.7 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 with Na2SO4 rejection rate of 99.4 %. This synergistic strategy of GQDs regulation on PA nanofilm structure and Arg post-treatment modification may provide a reliable and efficient technique for the design of NF membranes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water shortage is a serious resource problem facing the world today. According to the “World Water Development Report”, global water consumption has accelerated in the past 100 years, and the demand for water resources is steadily increasing at a rate of 1 % per year [1]. Membrane separation technique is crucial to explore unconventional resources for potable water supply. Nanofiltration (NF) has come into widespread use in wastewater treatment [2–4] and seawater desalination [5–7] on account of its low operating pressure and excellent separation performance. Permeance and selectivity are primary parameters in NF separation process [8] and maximizing both is the goal pursued by researchers. However, it is widely perceived that the growth in flux usually tends to cause a decline in rejection rate and vice versa. Therefore, how to overcome the permeance-selectivity limitation is still a tremendous challenge [9,10].

The NF composite membranes generally comprise polyamide (PA) functional layers and porous supporting materials. PA thin layers typically determine the separation performance of NF composite membranes [11], which are traditionally prepared by in-situ interfacial polymerization reaction [11,12]. Different reaction conditions would make direct impacts on the morphology and structure of the functional layers, thereby affecting the separation performance. Aiming at developing NF membranes with high performance, researchers are devoted to adjusting the intrinsic structure of PA nanofilms. For instance, reducing the thickness of the membranes [3,13–25], increasing the degree of membrane wrinkles [20,21], modifying the pore size of the membranes (including adding additional water pathways [13,22–24], creating a loose pore structure [25] etc.).

Through the application of different membrane technology, sub-10 nm or even sub-5 nm ultrathin separation membranes can be synthesized. Affected by the heterogeneous distribution and diffusion of monomers, the polyamide layers formed by traditional in situ interfacial polymerization are usually 100 nm thick [11,12]. The high thickness of separation layer will influence the transport of water molecules across the membrane, resulting in a low permeance below 10 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. The shorter water transport path improves the sub-10 nm membrane to possess more than twice as much permeance as before. Synthesis technologies such as regulated diffusion (permeance between 15 and 30 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, thickness about 12 nm [14]), layer-by-layer assembly (permeance about 19 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, thickness about 8 nm [15]) and support-free interface polymerization [18] all present excellent permeability.

Thickness is a major parameter affecting the permeance of NF membranes. A large quantity of attempts has been given to design ultrathin PA nanofilms, including molecular layer-by-layer deposition [15,16], electrostatic spray 3D printing [3,17], support-free interfacial polymerization [12,18], regulating the distribution and diffusion of monomers [12,19], etc. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is quasi-zero-dimensional nanomaterial, which has been used to tune PIP diffusion during the support-free interfacial polymerization [18]. A smooth PA functional layer as thin as 10.5 nm was readily obtained. The covalent bonding between PA matrix and GQDs ensures the defect-free formation of separation layers with remarkable Na2SO4 rejection. The water-salt selectivity breaks through the upper-bound limitation of traditional PA membranes [18]. The introduction of GQDs to prepare ultrathin and detect-free separation layer provides possibilities for further improvement of NF membranes.

Surface modification is a promising route to regulate the surface properties endowing NF membranes with better performance [26–30]. Peng et al. reported an approach using tetrakis (hydroxymethyl) phosphorous chloride (THPC) monomer during surface modification of PA separation layer [26]. The hydrophilicity effectively improved, and the permeance can reach 50.5 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 without apparent loss in inorganic salt rejection. Arginine (Arg) is an important component of aquaporins, which can promote the efficient transport of water molecules through the cell membranes. Chen et al. introduced this zwitterionic amino acids to modulate PA reverse osmosis (RO) membrane [27]. Arg was covalently connected to the membrane surface, so that the hydrophilicity and permeance were visibly improved. In a word, surface modification by hydrophilic molecules offers an effective and convenient method to improve the permeance of PA membranes.

Herein, a synergistic method for preparing defect-free NF membranes exhibiting ultra-high permeance and selectivity was proposed. GQDs enhanced PA (GPA) ultrathin nanofilms were synthesized at the free phase interface under the high content of GODs. The incorporation of GQDs can effectively control the pore size and obtain a highly permeable PA functional layer. Whereafter, Arg molecules were selected to adjust the surface and pore properties of the GPA NF composite membranes. Benefiting from GQDs enhancing and Arg engineering, the Arg-GPA NF membranes performed unprecedented separation behavior for desalination, which is superior to commercial NF membranes (permeance <10 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 [31]) and NF membranes reported in the literature (permeance about 15–35 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 [14,18–20,25,26,28]). This synergistic strategy of GQDs tuning PA matrix structure and Arg engineering promotes the Arg-GPA membranes to achieve a breakthrough of the permeance and selectivity limitation in NF separation process.

Commercial polyacrylonitrile (PAN) ultrafiltration membranes (permeance ≈ 300 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, molecular weight cutoff ≈ 150,000, pore size ≈ 25 nm) were purchased from Lanjing Membrane Technology Co. (Shangdong, China). Citric acid (CA), piperazine (PIP), trimesoyl chloride (TMC), arginine (Arg), n-heptane were bought from Aladdin Co. (Shanghai, China). Sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) were obtained from Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were acquired from Jiangtian Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). Different polyethylene glycol (PEG) were afforded by Heowns Biochemical Technology Co. (Tianjin, China). Deionized water for separation performance measurement was provided by Yongqingyuan Distilled Water Business Department (Tianjin, China). The GQDs used in the experiment were synthesized by the pyrolysis of CA, which had an average diameter of 2.5 nm and can be easily and uniformly dispersed in aqueous solution [18].

GQDs enhanced PA composite membranes were fabricated at aqueous-organic interface via support-free interfacial polymerization. Commercial PAN ultrafiltration membranes were pre-placed in an aqueous phase containing GQDs and PIP. PIP concentration was fixed at 0.125 g L−1. The n-heptane organic solution containing TMC was gently added to the aqueous surface, and then GPA nanofilms were rapidly formed at the interface. Subsequently, the nanofilms were deposited on the PAN substrate (a square with sides about 6 cm long) after elevation. GPA composite membranes were placed in 60 °C oven for 10 min to complete thermally treatment. GPA composite membranes were named as GPAx, where x indicates the concentration of GQDs (0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.30, 0.45, and 0.60 g L−1). The specific reaction conditions were listed in Table S1.

The GPA composite membranes were soaked in different concentrations of Arg aqueous solution (ranging from 2.5 to 15.0 g L−1) at 60 °C for different times (1.0, 4.0, 8.0, 12.0, and 24.0 h). After cleaning the membranes with deionized water, the Arg modified GPA composite membranes (Arg-GPA) were stored in a sealed plastic bag. Briefly, Arg-GPA composite membrane was named as Arg-GPAx-y, where y represents different modification conditions of Arg solutions (1 to 6 represents soaking solutions with different Arg concentrations for 24.0 h, 7 to 10 represents soaking in 10.0 g L−1 Arg solution for different times). The post-treatment modification conditions were summarized in Table S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GPA and Arg-GPA membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical structure of the GPA and Arg-GPA membranes was measured by using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Bruker, TENSOR II, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific, ESCALAB Xi+, British). The surface morphology was characterized by scanning electronic microscopy (SEM, Hitachi, Regulus 8100, Japan). The thickness and the root mean square roughness value (Rrms) were detected by atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker, Dimension Icon, Germany) after transferring onto the silicon wafer.

The pore sizes of the GPA and Arg-GPA NF membrane were calculated by the interception of neutral PEG solute. The content of PEG is assessed via total organic carbon analyzer (TOC, OI Analytical, 1030 W + 1088, America). The water contact angles and zeta potential were measured via contact angle goniometer (Powerach, JC2000D2, China) and electrokinetic analyzer (SurPASS 3, Anton Paar, Austria).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation performance of GPA and Arg-GPA membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The separation performance of GPA and Arg-GPA membranes was measured through a cross-flow equipment (effective permeable area is 2.54 cm2). The membrane samples were pre-pressed for 30 min under 2.5 bar. The measurement was carried out with a pressure of 2.0 bar. The flux (J
w , L m−2 h−1) and permeance (P
e , L m−2 h−1 bar−1) were calculated as follows:
(1)
J w
=
V
A Δ T
(2)
P e
=
J w
Δ P
where V (L) is the permeation volume, A (m2) is the effective permeable area, ΔT (h) is the permeation time, ΔP (bar) is the test pressure.

The salt rejection was obtained by rejecting 1000 ppm inorganic salts (Na2SO4, MgSO4, MgCl2, NaCl, respectively) aqueous solutions. The inorganic salt rejection rate (R, %) and Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity factor (α) were computed as follows:
(3)
R =
C f
−
C p
C f
× 100 %
(4)
α =
1 −
R NaCl
1 −
R
Na 2
SO 4
× 100 %
where Cf (ppm) and C
p (ppm) are the concentration of feed and permeate solution, which were measured by conductivity meter (Leichi, DDS-11A, China).

The pressure stability of Arg-GPA membranes was investigated by filtering 1000 ppm Na2SO4 aqueous solution under linearly increasing test pressure from 1.0 to 6.0 bar. The stability of Arg-GPA membranes under different concentration was evaluated with Na2SO4 solution of 500, 1000, 2000, 3000, 4000, and 5000 ppm as feed solution, respectively.

In order to detect the chemical stability, the Arg-GPA NF composite membranes were soaked in various harsh aqueous solutions (pH = 1.0 HCl solution, pH = 13.0 NaOH solution, 3 wt% H2O2 solution, 1000 ppm NaClO solution for 24.0, 48.0, and 72.0 h). After cleaning with deionized water, the permeance and Na2SO4 rejection rate were estimated via cross-flow equipment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure characterization and permselectivity of GPA membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The free-standing GPA nanofilms were fabricated at the interfacial area between an aqueous phase containing PIP with expected high content of GQDs and an organic phase containing TMC. The fabrication process and detailed information were presented in Fig. 1 and Table S1. As depicted in Fig. 1, GQDs participated in the interfacial polymerization reaction and were successfully integrated with the membrane matrix. The interfacial voids around the embedded GQDs are prone to act as molecular sieving channels [18,32]. Then GPA nanofilms were transferred atop porous substrates to prepare NF composite membranes. The chemical properties and physical structure of GPA membranes became stable after heat treatment.

Different from traditional in-situ interfacial polymerization, support-free interfacial polymerization is not influenced by the uneven distribution of monomers in substrate pores [18,33]. For purpose of investigating more accurate topography and structure parameters, the GPA nanofilms were assembled on PAN porous substrate for SEM measurement (Fig. 2a and d) and deposited on silicon wafer for AFM measurement (Fig. 2b and e). The GPA composite membrane presented a smooth morphology, without obvious defects and ridge-valley structure. More accurately, GPA1 nanofilm displayed small Rrms of 0.989 nm, and GPA2 nanofilm of 0.888 nm. The two-phase monomer diffuses uniformly at the aqueous-organic interface, and the rapid heat dissipation makes the interface more stable [13], which leads to a smoother surface. Such a surface with low roughness brings positive influence on mitigating the deposition of foulants.

The thickness of GPA nanofilms was further investigated by AFM. With the introduction of GQDs, GPA nanofilm possessed an ultrathin structure with thickness of 7.7 ± 0.4 nm for GPA1 and 5.9 ± 0.5 nm for GPA2 (Fig. 2c and f). After massively adding GQDs, the GPA nanofilm still maintains a thickness below 8.0 nm (Fig. 2g). More GQDs decelerates the diffusion of aqueous monomer through the two-phase interface, contributing to lower PIP monomer concentration at the interface and the formation of thinner nanofilm [18]. The uniform diffusion of PIP on the free interface and the steric hindrance from GQDs accelerates the termination of the interfacial polymerization reaction. This is a relatively simple and easy technique to synthesize ultrathin PA nanofilms even at high GQDs content in aqueous phase.

Carboxyl groups, hydroxyl groups, and epoxy groups in GQDs can participate in polymerization and form stable robust nanofilms [18,32]. The GPA nanofilms as functional separation layers were designed to prepare the NF composite membranes. When the GQDs concentration increased, the permeance of GPA composite membranes showed a substantial increase, from GPA1 membrane of 61.9 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 with Na2SO4 rejection rate of 96.6 %, up to GPA6 membrane of 361.8 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 with Na2SO4 rejection rate of 7.7 % (Fig. 2h). The large amount of GQDs effectively changes the internal structure of GPA nanofilms. The water channels in traditional PA membranes are generally comprised of two varieties of pores: network pore and aggregate pore [34]. The increment in dimension and quantity of network pores can enhance the water flux notably. In this study, as schematically illustrated in Fig. 1, water channels in the GPA nanofilms consist of three categories: PA channels from the reaction of PIP and TMC, GQDs-PA interfacial channels, and GQDs-GQDs interfacial channels. Among these various structures, the polyamide channels have the minimum pore sizes, which lead to ultra-high rejection of multivalent anions but lower permeance [18,35].

At a low concentration of GQDs in aqueous phase, the GQDs-PA interfacial channels are embedded in the PA functional layer [18,32], and the permeance of NF membranes increases while the rejection scarcely changes (such as the reported membrane exhibited a permeance of 32.1 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 with Na2SO4 rejection rate of 99.6 % [18]). In the case of further GQDs addition, the permeance of GPA1 and GPA2 membranes was gradually enhanced with slightly reduced Na2SO4 rejection, revealing the penetration of GQDs-PA interfacial channels. When the amount of GQDs reached a large contention in aqueous phase, the GQDs-GQDs interfacial channels would generate and gradually penetrate the functional layer, causing a dramatical increase in permeance and a substantial decline in inorganic salt rejection rate (lower than 90 %, from 78.3 % for GPA3 to 7.7 % for GPA6 membrane). The GQDs-GQDs interfacial pores with larger sizes observably facilitate water molecules and inorganic salt ions across the polyamide matrix, which greatly impairs the separation performance of GPA composite membranes.

GPA nanofilms with ultra-high permeance were reliably fabricated by a simple support-free interfacial polymerization method. GQDs-PA interface channels and GQDs-GQDs interface channels resulted in larger pore sizes with high permeation flux and low interception performance (Fig. 1). GPA NF composite membranes with devisable separative capacity are of vital importance in the potential dye/inorganic salt separation for reuse of wastewater [3,36,37]. It is well-known that NF membranes with ultra-high permselectivity are the focus of scientific research and industrial application [10,18,38,39]. Unfortunately, the GPA membrane cannot maintain ideal ultra-high rejection. Given this circumstance, Arg engineering was creatively designed to improve the retention behavior of GPA membrane for highly effective desalination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure characterization and properties of the Arg-GPA membranes
ce:section.ce:para: To further develop ultra-high permselectivity NF membranes with GPA ultrathin nanofilms as functional separation layers, Arg molecules were utilized to carry out the post-treatment modification. Arg is an important component of aquaporins, containing hydrophilic groups of an amino group, a carboxyl group, and a guanidine group, which can promote the efficient transfer of water molecules [40]. The hydrophilicity of membranes can be extremely improved by grafting Arg molecules [27].

The GPA composite membrane was directly soaked in Arg solution to obtain Arg-GPA composite membrane. As shown in Fig. 3 , Arg molecules could react with the remaining acyl chloride groups and carboxyl groups at the entrance of water channels and on the surface of membrane. Part of carboxyl groups were contained in GQDs and another were hydrolyzed by the residual acyl chloride groups of TMC [18,27]. FTIR has first measured the changes in chemical bonds of GPA membrane and Arg-GPA membranes (Fig. S2). The CO bond strength of the Arg-GPA membrane increased significantly, indicating that the Arg grafting process successfully formed OCN structure.

The morphology and thickness of Arg-GPA membrane were further estimated by SEM (Fig. 4a) and AFM (Fig. 4b and c). The surface of Arg-GPA membrane also appeared a smooth morphology without obvious defects, which proves that the Arg engineering has no great impact on the smooth structure of the surface. Arg engineered nanofilms were transferred to silicon wafers for thickness measurement after dissolving PAN substrate by polar organic solvent. According to Fig. 4c, the thickness of Arg-GPA1-4 was increased to ≈11.2 ± 0.6 nm. The probable reasons might be the increased thickness caused by Arg grafting. In addition, the modification has no adverse impact on the surface roughness (Rrms ≈ 0.810 nm).

Elemental analysis by XPS spectroscopy manifested that the content of N element increased from 13.59 % of GPA1 to 17.18 % of Arg-GPA1-4, which is consistent with the FTIR results. Compared with the percentages of characteristic peak areas before Arg engineering, an obvious increase in the content of amide bonds and carboxyl groups from 11.1 % of GPA1 to 15.1 % of Arg-GPA1-4 was observed, which accounts for the successful grafting of Arg molecules.

The pore diameter and surface properties of Arg-GPA membrane were further measured to verify the changes in the microstructure after Arg engineering. According to Fig. 4h, compared with GPA1 membrane (MWCO ≈ 932 Da, pore size ≈ 0.682 nm), Arg-GPA1-4 membrane presented smaller MWCO of 762 Da and narrower pore size of 0.639 nm. It suggests that Arg grafting not only happened on the membrane surface, but also occurred at the entrance of water channels narrowing the pores of water transport ways. Furthermore, the molecular structure of Arg has hydrophilic groups, which can alleviate the consumption of carboxyl groups on hydrophilicity. At the same time, because the guanidine groups are positively charged in water, Arg-GPA1-4 membrane had smaller water contact angle of 51.2° and weaker electronegativity of −41.1 mV than GPA1 membrane (water contact angle of 54.3° and zeta potential of −81.3 mV). Although the weakening of the negative charge may reduce the Donnan effect, the size-dependent sieving is conducive to the rejection of inorganic salt ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Permeance and selectivity of Arg-GPA NF composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: A series of experiments of Arg engineering was operated with GPA1 membranes and the detailed separation performance data such as permeance and selectivity were given in Table 1 . When the Arg concentration elevated from 2.5 to 15.0 g L−1, the permeance of Arg-GPA1 membrane decreased from 61.9 to 39.5 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. From Arg-GPA1-3 to Arg-GPA1-6, because of the limited number of carboxyl sites on the membrane, excessive Arg molecules cannot be sufficiently grafted, so that the separation performance of the membrane no longer changes extremely under higher Arg concentration. Similarly, the permeance of Arg-GPA membrane presented a diminishing trend with the longer immersion time. Concomitantly, after a certain soaking time, the permeance will not change significantly (around 40.0 L m−2 h−1 bar−1). Among all Arg engineering conditions, Arg-GPA1-4 performed the best and stable separation permeance of 40.3 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. As Arg soaking time and the Arg concentration increased, more and more Arg molecules are bound to the surface which would narrow the pore size of water channels. Arg engineering efficiently reduces the loss of permeance caused by pore size reduction, and ensures the formation of high permeance membranes.

Membrane researchers always pursue perfect selectivity through continuous improvement. Based on size sieving and Donnan exclusion, the Na2SO4 rejection rate via NF separation process can achieve 99.0 % [18,32,41,42]. The grafting of Arg reduces the pore size, which is advantageous to the improvement of inorganic salt rejection. Although the introduction of Arg molecules slightly alleviated the negative electricity, the overall membrane surface still exhibited negative charge, which has a good repelling effect on SO4 2− ions and is conducive to the rejection of Na2SO4. The Arg-GPA membranes fabricated under all reaction conditions and the inorganic salt ions rejection rate were listed in Table S4. Arg-GPA1-4 membrane performed ultra-high rejection rate of Na2SO4 reaching 99.7 %. Moreover, the Arg-GPA1-4 membrane can allow NaCl permeation, in as much as Na+ and Cl− hold low hydration radiuses (0.358 and 0.332 nm [43]), but is highly repulsive to Na2SO4. Hence, Arg-GPA1-4 membrane demonstrated ultra-high Cl-/SO4 2− selectivity factor up to 309.5, which surpasses the permeance and selectivity limitation in NF separation process.

GPA2 to GPA6 membranes were also modified at the optimized post-treatment modification (Table S4). Arg-GPA2-1 membrane possessed ultra-high permeance of 60.7 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 with Na2SO4 rejection rate of 99.4 %, which is one of the most competitive performances among all reported NF membranes (Fig. 5e). In virtue of the existence of large pore size from the penetrated interfacial channels, the Na2SO4 rejection rate merely decreased to 88.5 % of Arg-GPA3-1 and 60.3 % of Arg-GPA4-1. Considering the aim of ultra-high performance after Arg post-treatment modification, Arg-GPA1-4 and Arg-GPA2-1 membranes were specially selected for the follow-up stability research.

Arg-GPA1-4 membrane retained excellent pressure resistance from 1.0 to 6.0 bar (Fig. 5a), and Arg-GPA2-1 maintained steady separation performance below 4.0 bar (Fig. 5b). As the operating pressure rose, the flux of the Arg-GPA membranes increased linearly, which is the common relationship between flux and pressure in NF operation. Concentration polarization under high flux condition is the probable reason for the decline of salt rejection for Arg-GPA2-1 NF membrane. In Fig. 5c and d, the Na2SO4 rejection rate of Arg-GPA1-4 and Arg-GPA2-1 membranes showed a downward trend with the strengthening of the electrostatic shielding effect at high inorganic salt concentration. Whereas, Arg-GPA1-4 can also preserve a Na2SO4 rejection rate of 85.6 % even at the salt concentration of 5000 ppm. With higher concentration of inorganic salts and operation pressure, more cations attached to the membrane surface, causing the weakening of Donnan electric potential of the ultrathin nanofilm separation layer, thereby reducing the repulsion effect and rejection rate [44–46]. Further work is needed to overcome this weakness for ultrathin membranes.

In extensive comparison with the progressive NF membranes reported (Fig. 5e), the accurate values were listed in Table S7, Arg-GPA1-4 and Arg-GPA2-1 NF composite membranes have superior permeance under the same ultra-high Na2SO4 rejection rate (>99.4 %). As illustrated in Fig. 1, GQDs-PA interfacial channels and GQDs-GQDs interfacial channels promote water molecules passing through the membrane. Furthermore, the post-treatment modification of Arg engineering shown in Fig. 3 not only intensify the hydrophilic properties of the membrane, but also compensate for the larger pore sizes coming from interfacial channels, which enhance the rejection of Na2SO4 while maintaining ultra-high permeance. It was a noticeable improvement benefited from the synergistic effect that the ultra-high permeance and rejection rate of Arg-GPA1-4 and Arg-GPA2-1 surpassed the reported NF membranes in Fig. 5e. In Fig. 5f, Arg-GPA1-4 membrane kept a stable permeance and Na2SO4 rejection during the 120-h long-term operation. This verified that Arg-GPA NF membrane is robust and defect-free, and adheres tightly to the substrate during the filtration process without peeling and breaking.

The functional layer of PA NF membrane is prone to degradation in acid, alkali, and oxidant environments [47–51]. Arg-GPA1-4 membrane treated with acid and oxidant solution still maintained a stable Na2SO4 rejection rate of 99.6 % and slightly fluctuated permeance, which was shown in Table S5, except that Na2SO4 rejection rate decreased to 98.8 % after soaking in NaOH solution. The synthesis of ultrathin nanofilms with the application of GODs provided abundant interfacial channels for high permeance. Arg engineering guaranteed the ultrathin thickness and simultaneously facilitated the water molecules across the membrane while adjusting the pore sizes. As expected, the Arg-GPA composite membranes can break through the permeance-selectivity limitation of NF membranes reported in recent literature (Table S7). The strategy of GQDs enhancement and Arg post-treatment modification has great significance for developing advanced membranes with ultra-high desalination performance.

GQDs enhanced ultrathin nanofilms were readily fabricated by support-free interfacial polymerization. The formation of GQDs-PA interfacial channels and GQDs-GQDs interfacial channels dramatically increased the permeance of GPA membranes. Subsequently, post-treatment modification was elaborately designed to develop NF composite membranes with ultra-high separation performance. The grafting of Arg provides more hydrophilic groups and adjusts the pore sizes of water channels. Under synergistic regulation of GQDs and Arg on nanofilm thickness, pore size, surface electrical properties, and so forth, the optimized Arg-GPA NF membrane possessed an ultra-high permeance of 40.3 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 with Na2SO4 rejection rate of 99.7 %, and prominent Cl−/SO4 2− selectivity factor of 309.5. Especially, Arg-GPA membranes displayed excellent antioxidant and anti-acid stability, while still maintaining excellent separation performance during long-term operation. This synergy method of GQDs enhancement and Arg engineering is scalable for the design of innovative membranes in desalination process.

Jiawei Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Experimental, Software, Data analysis, Diagram, Writing. Zhen Wang: Experimental, Writing. Wenwen Li: Experimental, Writing. Xuliang Wang: Theoretical analysis, Supervision. Yanlei Su: Conceptualization, Theoretical analysis, Project design, Final polishing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21878217).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.desal.2022.116232.
